speaker: interviewer
text: Seven.

speaker: interviewee
text: The little girl, Helen, a neighbor came in and told while Helen went to fetch potatoes, she told me that her parents were killed. She comes from a nearby village. And so she worked for Mrs. Winer. But the boys up on the hill where she goes to pasture the cows know that she's Jewish. And they are tormenting her, threatening to expose her. So Mrs. Winer, who is a kind woman, is trying to find a place for her. So I am her placement. But when Helen-- and now, after-- now knowing that she's Jewish, I looked at her in different light. When evening came, we had supper. I was to sleep with her in the kitchen. It was like a bench that opens up like a bed. She knelt and said her prayers. I did the same thing to be careful.

speaker: interviewee
text: The next morning, Mrs. Winer said, in order to have ration cards, you have to go with-- present your documents and register. And then you get ration cards. So on the whole night, I was on my mind, I have to present my documents. How do I know that they are real, that they are identical, that there's not going to be any doubt? I never presented them officially.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't sleep that night. In the morning, I couldn't even eat breakfast. She gave me a can of milk to deliver to some people there. There was a church to deliver. And from there, she described direction I go. I'm going into the hallway with my documents. And I have to open a door to get in where that office is. I'm freezing. I'm withdrawing myself. But somebody sees me and notices my fear. So I know I have to walk in.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I'm walking in. And I said, I am staying with my aunt for several weeks, and I need to register. A young Ukrainian in the office takes my documents. He looks at them. I'm frozen.

speaker: interviewee
text: The telephone was in the next room. He has to go in. And some [? nice ?] [? here ?] German-- he works there; maybe he's in charge-- comes over and picks up my documents. He puts it down. The fellow comes back. He looks at me. He see-- he take-- he takes a form, stamps it, and give me my ration cards. I'm registered there. It went very easy. I walked out, and I'm hugging those documents. This is my passageway to life. And while I'm walking the street, going back more encouraged, I see Jews are being led by Ukrainian militia, by guard, crying, shema Yisrael, crying because they dug-- dig them out of hiding places.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I am like, for one, dangerous. But I have the documents. And I'm walking like a Christian, and I'm nervous. I can't wait to get back to Mrs. Winer's.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came back to Mrs. Winer's, the little girl was gone. I knew already what happening. But she did not tell me, and I did not ask. And she told me, it's-- the weather is nice. You are to take the cow up to pasture. It's-- to her garden is like a path with a gate. And it's a hilly area with grass. They're grazing there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- [PAUSES] I go into the stall, and the cow is chained. And I was never close to a cow. I'm afraid, mortally afraid. But if I want to live, I have to do it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I unchain. And she gave me a prod. And I did not wear my good shoes of my brother's but old shoes. And I go up the hill, chasing the cow. The cow already knows her way. She goes up, and I come up to pasture there. There are many boys pasturing their cow grazing there, and they saw-- recognized that there was a black-and-white patches cow. They know the cow. And they see a young girl-- not anymore that little girl that they tormented.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one of them, Romik, comes over. He said, that Mrs.-- Mrs. Winer's cow. You are relative of hers? Because I didn't look the part, like a pasturing cows. They provided me with a lie. Yes, I said, she's my aunt. And they said, you know the girl she had? She's Jewish. I said, my aunt didn't know, and she sent her away. And they started talking. And when my cow strayed away, they brought her back, and so-- which was good.

speaker: interviewee
text: I brought the cow back. Now she said I have to bring water, fetch water from a well down below. So I went. She had like a board with pails to carry. It went down. Fetched the water. The cow sucks out a pail of water in a minute. And cleaned away the stall from the manure there. She puts it in a-- there's a box, which they use as fertilizer. And I went. Came into the house. So far, so good. This was daytime, so at lunch time, is interrupting. And afternoon, I go again. I helped her with the kitchen. She had those bordering girl helped [? her ?] [? that. ?] And then afternoon, I come up again to the hill. [PAUSES] For some times, I worked. Then everything was fine. The boys were my friends. One time, I went into the stall. And I said was attaching the chain, a fly must've bit the cow. And she like moved and stepped on my toe with all her weight, with the hoof on my foot. And I-- the more I tried to free myself, the deeper she embedded her foot into-- her hoof into my foot. Immediately, when I pushed her, my foot was covered with blood, mixed with a manure there, which caused an infection. And I limped into the house. And when [? we see it-- ?] then when I washed it away, and put some bandages on. But it was hurting me. And the next day, I cannot go-- I could not go to the pasture. In fact, she went. She was sitting because the cow-- even though they give out grain and other thing-- needs the grass.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of the boys, knowing the cow, they know it's Mrs. Winer. They walked over, and they said, how's your niece? Why is she not here?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she, not realizing, says, she's not my niece. She works for me. The boys realized that I told a lie. As primitive as they were, they were clever. I stayed away for some time. And I was very upset because if I can-- she cannot use my services, she's going to send me away. But she didn't. And then she kept the cow in the stall and-- and did not take her up any more. After two weeks, it healed because it was infected. She did not call a doctor-- it was very badly infected-- because perhaps she suspected I'm Jewish. She didn't want any exposure for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I came back on the hill already. And the boy, Miron-- he was my tormentor-- he came over. He said, hi, Paulina. How's your aunt? I said, she's fine. He said, you are liar. She's not your aunt. You are Jewish, and you are working for her. Otherwise, you don't look a-- like a peasant. Otherwise, why would you pasture a cow? I started crying. I said, they're taking Polish girls to forced labor to Germany. And I didn't want to go because my stepmother wants to get rid of me. Is it a crime? And I cried.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they didn't buy that. They didn't believe it. And they walked away. And I said, I know I'm in danger. I have nobody to talk to. Now, I-- Mrs. Popel came to see me a few times while I was pasturing the cow. [PAUSES] And now, they-- and I was afraid of them. Now, I have to go up. I know they're suspecting me. There was one boy, Vladic-- handsome boy, but primitive. He was a watch-- night watchman, and at daytime, he pastured his parents' cows. He noticed me. And when I looked in his direction, he pretend he doesn't see me. When my cow strayed away, he rounded up, brought her back. And slowly, slowly, he sat down. And he started talking to me. He was my friend. The boys suspecting me and were plotting. And he knew about it. And he became my protector. Seemed after a while he brought me [? fruit ?] from their garden. And I told him, because I don't want to go to Germany, they just suspecting me and that. But they did suspect me. And I was afraid that they're going to report me. At one time, I'm sitting. She's told-- stay a little longer because a day before it rained, so she should have more from the pasture-- more grass. I stayed longer. And Vladic came over. And he sat down. And he-- something he had in his pocket. He like fumbling in his pocket. And he took out. And I see a gun. I was frightened. So I grabbed him and held his hands so closely, he shouldn't-- I said, don't shoot me. Don't shoot me. I like you. You're my friend.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was in disbelief. You thought that I would hurt you? It seems that he loved me already because he was watching me for some time.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, the boys are plotting to report you, and I am warning them that they should-- they should not. I don't care for my life. If they should harm you, they'll pay a price. They know I have a gun. I just wanted to show you that I am protecting you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I'm still bluffing. And I said, why would they want to report me? I'm not Jewish. I told them.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, Paulina, be quiet. Don't talk. Because he knew. And he held me and was very comforting. I was all shook up. And then it was time for me to go. And I remember there was a puddle down the hill. He picked me up and carried me across it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I realized that he cares for me. In his primitive way, he loved me. But could I afford any emotions such as that? I came back, and the whole night I was restless because I know the boys suspect me. He's my protector, but are they going to go to report me? What should I do? I lied awake, thinking, should I run? If they go to the police, I'm finished. I-- I don't know my papers are [? correct. ?] Where should I go? I have no place to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were saying on that hilly area, there was like a dense growth of small trees. And the boys were saying that there were some Jews in hiding. At night, they go into the farms and steal. And one of these days, they're going to round them up. And one day, I was sitting up on a hill with my cow, getting used to, still thinking for those boys. And this fellow, Vladic-- became my protector-- he sat near me. And out of respect-- because he was older-- they didn't go near. And he warned them that if they are going to harm me, he's going to kill them. He doesn't care. So they were sort of quiet. [PAUSES] One day, as we sitting, up from up the hill we could see the road. And we see a German car stopping, a Jeep-- like a Jeep type-- two Germans with machine guns going up through the hill. Immediately, I think that they're coming to get me because the boys reported me. And I'm freezing. I'm dead. Vladic was not there. They go-- instead of coming towards my direction, they made a right and went into the woods, those dense pine trees. And we heard machine guns. Shooting, shooting, shooting, and it stopped. And then, because I was up the hill, I was able to see them going back and going into their car. I felt so exposed and afraid. I did not know what happened. And boys from down were calling-- calling [NON-ENGLISH], this one, calling, come down. There's some killed Jews. There's a killed dead-- dead Jew here. They should come down.

speaker: interviewee
text: So some of the boys who suspecting me say, Paulina, you want to come with us? I said, no, I have to watch the cow. He said, no, no, no, the cow is not going to go to a place. Just for a few minutes, go down. So not to be-- not to be suspected, I went down. As we were walking down, there was a young-- in his 20s-- a boy, man, stretched out, bleeding by his neck, curly hair. He-- because he was hiding, he wasn't shaving, with a beard. But you could see nice features. Skinny, because they were starving, hiding.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were looking. And they were watching me, those boys. And out of the woods, of the bushes, there was a rustle. And a girl came out, a young girl, even though she was in danger. But when she realized her brother shot, she didn't care. She threw herself on the body of that boy. Starting screaming, [NON-ENGLISH], my dear brother. After all this time-- and crying and weeping. And one of the boys-- those-- said, we could still go and call the-- call the Germans back-- back to have her killed too. There was an older woman who had tears. And she said, don't do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know who Jesus Christ was? Jesus was a Jew. You know that, don't you? And they are his people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't you do that. You don't know what tomorrow brings to us. And she-- the boy like put his head down [? and he ?] [? sat. ?] And then the boys went back, and they were watching me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, when they came up on the hill-- and I had to be strong. And they said, well, you feel sorry for the Jews? You feel sorry for them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, well-- like any girl would say-- yes, I'm sorry. I went to school with many Jewish girls. They were very nice. I think it's terribly cruel what the Germans are doing, killing innocent people.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one of the boys said-- [NON-ENGLISH], this boy-- and he was, but we are not doing it. The Germans are doing it. And I was thinking, what were they doing to me?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that night, I did not sleep. I-- I couldn't-- I was upset. After supper I went in the kitchen. I was-- in that-- I was like getting ready to go. The door was lightly open, and Mrs. Winer was talking to her daughter. They were talking. They say [? they were-- ?] that they discovered hiding places. Christians-- they killed the Christians too. And she-- she knew these people. It's a small town. She said she was marveling that they survived all this time. And Mrs. Winer said, if that war-- they shot the Christians and the Jews, to set an example. So Mrs. Winer said, if that war lasts longer, there wouldn't be any Jews in Europe. And at night, everybody went to sleep. It hit me hard. I knew all that. But hearing it from those women in the next room hit me hard. There'll be no Jews. I'll be all alone in this world.

speaker: interviewee
text: Why am I fighting to live? There'll be no Jewish people. But I cannot bear the thought of being shot and killed and being put to death. I want to live.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to bed, my heart-- my head burning. And what occurred with the boys, I was afraid that they suspect me. That's why they took me to that grave to see my reaction.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I always had my good shoes next to my bed. And there was a back door with a window, which if I will hear by the latch Germans coming in, I'm going to run through the back door. Where am I going to run? Into the woods, where that girl is.

speaker: interviewee
text: One-- one-- next day, it rained. And I had to fetch water and do laundry. So I had that board across my shoulders, with the pails, with chain. I go to the well, where there's a pail attached. And you roll down the water. It fills up, and then it comes up. I unhook and fill my pails. And I was going to fetch water. And I'm satisfied that I don't have to go up on the hill to face the boys. Suddenly, at the gate, I see two Germans in uniform, fumbling with the latch. They're trying to get in. I froze. They're coming for me. And I'm at the-- at a entrance door, standing there. And they're coming towards me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they ask, is your mother home? I couldn't speak because I was frozen. And they thought that I don't understand German. So I follow them because Mrs. Winer was stripping the beds for the laundry to wash in that last room where the girls were. So I went in. And I said, Mrs. Winer, there are some Germans here. They want to see you. And she said, what do they want? And she walked out. And she spoke fluently German have. So her husband was a judge, and she was educated. And they were impressed by her German-- fluence in her German language. And she said that she went to college, and her husband was a judge and spoke German.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, I apologize, but we are rounding up Jews. [? Now ?] they're in hiding, and we'll have to search your house, apologizing. And I'm standing there. And Mrs. Winer looked at me. And I was afraid I'm betraying myself. I'm frozen. I'm afraid. And they're going from room to room. Even-- they even went to the stall to check. And then they came back, satisfied. And they said, you must not let in any Jews or hide-- give shelter because you know what will happen to you and your daughter. And they left. All that affected me so that I avoided Mrs. Winer's eyes. And I ran into the stall, where I threw up. And tears and vomit together. And I started wiping myself and cleaning away the straw, that she shouldn't see. And Mrs. Winer was yelling-- Paulina was my name. Paulina, we wasted time. Go fetch the water because we have to do the laundry.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I carried the pail with that board across. And I saw the Germans across the road, going into-- from house to house. Even though they left already, I felt exposed. I wished to be indoors.

speaker: interviewee
text: I carried the water. It was heavy on my shoulder. And I climbed up the hill. And from the tension and from the fear, driven by fear, I carried-- my-- my legs knuckling under me from fear. And I came. And I applied my energy washing the laundry and scrubbing. And then we hung it out for drying.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was grateful that I was indoors. And I was thinking of that girl in the woods. What's happening? Maybe they're going to come. There might be more Jews killed and all that.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES]

speaker: interviewee
text: This fellow Vladic was my friend, and I knew. But I knew that I'm in danger. But where should I run?

speaker: interviewee
text: If I would hide in the woods, how much-- it's not a object you will put it down, and it lays there. I have to eat. It rains. A rain like that, I was thinking of that sister laying there, soaked, wet, hungry. But there is no way that I could go or bring food because she does not know I am her friend. I can be seen. It's uphill. So there's nothing to do. In spite of all what I went through, I felt fortunate. I have a roof over my head. And I am safe. Mrs. Popel was-- as I said that my brothers knew where I am, and they gave me those shoes. Occasionally, Mrs.-- Mrs. Popel had mail for me, she brought to me. And she was my friend. Now, there was another problem. There was a registration of the whole Polish Christian population to determine, up to the third generation, known Jews. If somebody was half Jew, they were considered Jews. A new registration, where you have to prove with your birth certificate from the church where we were christened to-- where they prove that you are-- you are Christian, of Christian origin.

speaker: interviewee
text: My documents are not valid anymore. So what should I do? I'm upset.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, at one time, I came to see Mrs. Popel to ask her if she heard-- if she had a letter from my brothers because my brothers knew where I am. She said no. But she told me not to come around for my safety because they're searching houses now, from door to door. And I cannot come. I walked back very sad because she was my friend, and I don't even have her. And I told her about the documents. And she said, you give me your birth certificate and your documents. I'm going to go-- she's a Ukrainian, which are Greek Catholic. But she's going to go to [NON-ENGLISH] to the Polish priest. She's prominent. Perhaps they know her. And she's going to present my papers. She'll say, I'm sick. And on the basis of my birth certificate and christening from the church and all that, they'll give me a document. She went there. And she obtained it. And I kissed her and hugged her. She went there, and I was to know whether she was successful. Because she was so prominent, they didn't even question and doubt. And that evening, after I put the cow and everything was taken care-- I asked Mrs. Winer's permission, I'm going to see Mrs. Popel. Hania was being quiet already. Mrs. Popel took me into her room and hugged me. She said, it was like [? bop-- ?] like [INAUDIBLE] so easy. She said, he didn't asked question because he knows me. And so he stamped it. I have a paper. And we kissed and hugged. She said, that calls for a celebration. She took us some wine. We drank, and I left. Having those document-- I said, I have my birth certificate. I have this paper. I have that. I'm safe. I slept better that night. Now, I told Mrs. Winer-- Mrs. Winer kept asking me. I said, I wrote to my stepfather-- stepmother. Told my father to send it to me, this paper, there. Two weeks left before the deadline.

speaker: interviewee
text: I showed her. She said, you go tomorrow morning to register. And the whole day I'm thinking, I have to present my documents tomorrow. How do I know that they are real? They gave me-- now-- [? no, ?] [? now what ?] am I saying? So we have to present those documents, and you have to-- they told me-- I-- I went because I already had-- was registered. They told me I have to provide a photograph. They took those documents that I had. And in few days, when I have a photo to bring it, where they going to put it on, on that new document.

speaker: interviewee
text: I waited anxiously. The document came, my Kennkarte. And I felt I have sort of a-- my lifeline is extended. And with those documents, somehow, I felt I'm convincing the boys. And in meantime, the front kept coming closer. And Mrs. Winer was thinking of leaving town because they're going to evacuate. We already knew that so many, so many kilometer, the Germans were retreating. And Mrs. Winer is to go-- go on the German side. So she sold the cow, and she's liquidating-- packing up everything, putting in the attic there. And now I won't have any employment. And Mrs. Popel has to leave, too. She's evacuating. And she is-- she was only crying that she has her little dog is going to suffer when they have a hardships and so. We came-- I came to say goodbye to her. And she's now sending Hania, the girl, away.

speaker: interviewee
text: So Hania, I thought she has family. She didn't have anybody. So where is Hania going to go? And she cannot stay in her apartment because the Germans were confiscating these apartments, and the front line is coming. So Mrs. Winer said, how about you looking for a little place, apartment-- you shack up with Hania? And she said, you could take some parts of the furniture. So Hania went, was the spoke girl.

speaker: interviewee
text: She went and spoke-- and looked for an apartment. And we found a room by a lady-- she lived upstairs-- a widow. We found a room. Mrs. Popel gave us some furniture we could drag over. And we moved in. And the city was being evacuated, but we stayed on. And the Germans were retreating. And they had a military kitchen there. And we found out that [PAUSES] they need girls for peeling potatoes. There is like a general staff there because it's close to the front. They need girls to peel potatoes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I asked some girls, could we work there too? Yes. So we left the apartment, and we went there. And we were peeling potatoes. And when there was an air raid, the Germans ran, but we had to stay.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was, from the general staff, an officer, Richter. Near Cologne, he was-- he was an aristocrat. Probably, he was not a Nazi, but he had high ranking. And a girl told me-- in the meantime, from all those-- from all that nerves and tiredness, I had pain. I had pain. I thought, it's an ulcer. I was throwing up. I was growing thin. I was sick. I couldn't go peel potato. I was laying in bed. And the girls called in-- there was a military doctor. He came to see me. He said it looks like my gall bladder.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, what are you girls doing here in the front line? This is dangerous. This is not-- there no more civilians. It's military territory.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said that my father was taken by the Russians to Siberia-- lying through my teeth. I have nobody, so I'm staying. And he said, you should not be in the front line. There were aerial fights, shooting. And the German personnel there went to a bunker to hide. We were still sitting, peeling potatoes. And the bullets were flying and bombs exploding. And somebody said-- one of the girls said, then there is, from the general staff that's there, there's one, he called Richter. He wants to get a girl to send to his parents-- a German girl. They couldn't get any help in the house. He lives near Cologne. And perhaps I would go-- I would go to Germany. They were rounding-- and he would include me in a Polish transport. They were rounding up girls to go to labor to Poland. They could include me in that transport to go to Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I wanted to be on that territory because if my brothers have survive, and the Russians come forward, then I can unite with my brothers. I don't know if they're alive. If I go to Germany, I'm distancing myself. To me, Russia is now my liberator. If the Germans retreat, and I'm remained in that territory, I'll find out about my brothers. I don't want to go away.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was impossible because it was the front line. The bombs and air raids and machine gunning and artillery-- I wouldn't survive. And I was not feeling good. I had pain, so it would have been better for me. I'm going to die here. And I figure, if I survive the war, we had an uncle in Switzerland, which is a neutral country. After the war, through him, we will find each other. If my brothers survive, we'll find him, and I must go. I should go to Germany. In Germany, they don't know me. I'm a Polish Christian.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to see this Herr Richter. He was high ranking. And he was a-- at first, I tried not to speak German well because Christians didn't speak so well. Jews, because it's similar to the jargon of the Jewish language-- but in order to communicate with him, I had to speak. He was impressed, I speak well in German.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, there's a transport from Lemberg going of Polish girls. He's going to take me there. I'm going to go. And it was decided that next day, I'll get my stuff. And he's going to bring me to Lemberg, where he's going to include me into a transport that goes from Polish girls that they send to Germany. I said goodbye to Hania and to those girls that were peeling potatoes. The next day, he put me on a truck with soldiers-- that covered wagon trucks. And he, jokingly, Mr. Richter said, boys, behave. And we drove to Germany. And there were-- he and another high-ranking officer followed in a-- in a car to Lemberg. We arrived to Lemberg. And he cannot go right away to that registration place. He has to bring me in the German quarters there, where those military go in. At the door, they said, she's a civilian. She cannot go in. And because he was so high ranking, he explained he's sending me to his mother. It's a temporary-- he said, if there is an air raid, she cannot go in-- go into the air-- to the cellar, he's going to be in trouble.
